coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-free ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal determination of glyphosate based on copper nanoclusters (ZIF/CuNCs) combined with blue carbon dots (bCDs)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huiwen
Liu, Xukai
Wang, Xiulin
Qiu, Ping
Li, Pengjun
coredata.dc:description:
A novel ratio fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform for detecting glyphosate based on blue carbon dots (bCDs) combined with ZIF/CuNCs nanomaterials that encapsulate copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) in a metal-organic framework (MOF). In principle, the immobilization of Cu2+ in ZIF/CuNCs results in complexation with imidazole in ZIF, leading to fluorescence quenching of ZIF/CuNCs, while the reference fluorophore bCDs remains unaffected. In addition, the colorimetric sensing strategy was based on the efficient peroxidase-like activity of bCDs binding to Cu2+, catalyzing H2O2 to generate
OH. Under this condition, TMB could be oxidized to form blue oxTMB. However, when glyphosate was involved in the system, the fluorescence of ZIF/CuNCs was restored upon due to the strong chelation between Cu2+ and glyphosate, while the peroxidase-like activity of bCDs/Cu2+ decreased and resulted in the generation of fewer oxTMB, accompanied by a lighter blue color. The sensing platform was successfully applied to the determination of glyphosate in real samples of lake water and cabbage, demonstrating reliable and sensitive performance in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratio fluorescent
Colorimetric
Dual-signal
Carbon dots
ZIF/CuNCs
Glyphosate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantification of glyphosate in real samples
Quantitative analysis of glyphosate with dual signal sensors
Selective exploration of dual-signal sensing platforms

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21765015 , 21808099 ), China and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province ( 20192BCD40001 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of ZIF/CuNCs. (B) XPS survey of ZIF/CuNCs. XPS high-resolution survey of Zn 2p (C), Cu 2p (D). (E) Zeta potential of, ZIF and ZIF/CuNCs. (F) FTIR spectra of CuNCs, ZIF, and ZIF/CuNCs. (G) Comparison of the fluorescence of CuNCs and ZIF/CuNCs. (H) Fluorescence emission spectra of bCDs at excitation wavelengths of 300–360 nm.

(A) Lineweaver-Burk plots for different TMB concentrations, and (B) for different H2O2 concentrations. (C) Lifetimes of ZIF/CuNCs in the absence (black line) and presence (red line) of Cu2+. (D) The emission spectra of ZIF/CuNCs (blue curve) and UV absorption spectra of Cu2+ (red curve). (E) High-resolution Cu 2p energy spectra of the mixture of ZIF/CuNCs and Cu2+. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Feasibility investigation: (A) Fluorescence sensing of different systems, including (a) bCDs/ZIF/CuNCs, (b) bCDs/ZIF/CuNCs + glyphosate, (c) bCDs/ZIF/CuNCs + Cu2+ + glyphosate, (d) bCDs/ZIF/CuNCs + Cu2+, and (e) bCDs. (Inset: corresponding color changes under UV light.) (B) Colorimetric sensing of (a) bCDs + TMB, (b) Cu2+ + TMB, (c) bCDs/Cu2+ + TMB, and (d) bCDs/Cu2+ + glyphosate + TMB. (Inset: corresponding color changes under natural light.).

The investigation of the sensing platform includes: (A) Fluorescence spectra and (B) linear correlation plot of fluorescence sensing platform for different concentrations of glyphosate, inset: color change of system under UV light. (C) UV absorption spectra and (D) linear correlation plot of colorimetric sensing platform for different concentrations of glyphosate, inset: color change of system under natural light.
Selectivity of dual-signal sensor (A) fluorescence and (B) UV spectrophotometry.
Dual signal sensor for glyphosate ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry based on copper nanoclusters (ZIF/CuNCs) combined with blue carbon dots (bCDs) encapsulated in organic frameworks.
The comparison of the performance of different sensors for detecting organophosphorus pesticides.
Detection results of the spiked samples based on the proposed method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration unit of lake samples is g/L while cucumber and apple samples’ are g/kg.

head.ce:title: Enzyme-free ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal determination of glyphosate based on copper nanoclusters (ZIF/CuNCs) combined with blue carbon dots (bCDs)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A novel ratio fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform for detecting glyphosate based on blue carbon dots (bCDs) combined with ZIF/CuNCs nanomaterials that encapsulate copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) in a metal-organic framework (MOF). In principle, the immobilization of Cu2+ in ZIF/CuNCs results in complexation with imidazole in ZIF, leading to fluorescence quenching of ZIF/CuNCs, while the reference fluorophore bCDs remains unaffected. In addition, the colorimetric sensing strategy was based on the efficient peroxidase-like activity of bCDs binding to Cu2+, catalyzing H2O2 to generate
OH. Under this condition, TMB could be oxidized to form blue oxTMB. However, when glyphosate was involved in the system, the fluorescence of ZIF/CuNCs was restored upon due to the strong chelation between Cu2+ and glyphosate, while the peroxidase-like activity of bCDs/Cu2+ decreased and resulted in the generation of fewer oxTMB, accompanied by a lighter blue color. The sensing platform was successfully applied to the determination of glyphosate in real samples of lake water and cabbage, demonstrating reliable and sensitive performance in practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental and food safety are significant concerns [1,2]. As society continues to evolve and people pursue healthier lifestyles, the demand for high-quality food including fruits and vegetables is increasing, along with the need for higher volumes [3]. However, crop yield and quality can be compromised by pests and weeds [4,5]. To address the issue of controlling undesirable growth and pests in agricultural fields, professionals often rely on pesticides and herbicides [6–8]. However, the use of these chemicals can result in residues that have adverse effects on ecosystems and human health [9,10]. Glyphosate, the most widely used and effective herbicide [11,12], is of particular concern. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a sensing platform that can efficiently detect glyphosate with both high speed and sensitivity.

Traditional conventional methods such as Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) [13–15], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) technique [16,17], chromatography [18], mass spectrometry [19], electrochemical methods [20], etc., have been widely employed in the construction of sensors. However, these methods require advanced and expensive instrumentation, professional technical operations, and complex sample pre-treatment, making them high-precision but not practical for routine use [21–23]. These deficiencies limit the prospects of on-site real-time monitoring. Therefore, it is necessary to develop simple, low-cost, highly sensitive, and rapid methods for detecting glyphosate. In recent years, fluorescence and colorimetric methods have been proposed for fast, accurate real-time monitoring of glyphosate [24]. In our previous work, we developed a sensing platform for the rapid quantification of glyphosate based on ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets using an enzyme-free ratio fluorescence and colorimetry dual-reading method [25], with a detection limit as low as 0.49 ng/mL. Additionally, Luo et al. proposed a nano-enzyme-mediated multifunctional platform with multi-signal responses (colorimetry, fluorescence, and temperature) for visual, sensitive, and portable detection of glyphosate [26], while Wu's research group developed a nano-sensor constructed through the combination of CDs and Cu2+ that functions as a dual-mode visual sensor and an intelligent sensing film. This system can monitor glyphosate on the surface of vegetables and demonstrates excellent practicality [27].

Compared with noble metal nanoclusters, such as gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and silver nanoclusters (AgNCs), copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) have attracted more and more attention from researchers due to their low cost and easy availability of raw materials [28,29]. For example, Yang et al. synthesized polyethyleneimine-protected copper nanoclusters (PEI-CuNCs) for the detection of Cu2+, biological thiols and acetylcholinesterase (AChE), with a detection limit of AChE as low as 1.38 mU/mL [30]. Furthermore, Hu et al. optimized the emission of copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) by using a ZIF-8 encapsulation strategy, not only increasing the quantum yield, but also stabilizing the fluorescence performance and making it more responsive [31]. On this basis, Hu's research group improved the sensitivity of the fluorescence probe by 50 times when detecting H2O2 using ZIF-8-modified CuNCs, and this fluorescence probe could also detect the activity of glucose oxidase with a detection limit of 0.1 mU/L [32].

Inspired by the above observation, we designed a novel ratio fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform for detecting glyphosate based on ZIF/CuNCs nanomaterials combined with bCDs. As shown in Scheme 1 , the immobilisation of Cu2+ in ZIF/CuNCs results in complexation with imidazole in ZIF, leading to fluorescence quenching in ZIF/CuNCs, while the reference fluorophore bCDs remains unaffected. In addition, the colorimetric sensing strategy was based on the efficient peroxidase-like activity of bCDs binding to Cu2+, catalyzing H2O2 to generate
OH. Under this condition, TMB could be oxidized to form blue oxTMB. However, the fluorescence of ZIF/CuNCs was restored upon adding glyphosate due to the strong chelation between Cu2+ and glyphosate, while the peroxidase-like activity of bCDs/Cu2+ decreased. In conclusion, we have successfully constructed a dual-signal sensing platform for glyphosate detection using fluorescence and colorimetric methods.

Glyphosate, with a purity of 98.7 %, was procured from Pushrm Technology Ltd in Beijing, China. Zinc acetate (ZnAc2), 2-methylimidazole, and copper sulphate (CuSO4) were obtained from Macklin Biochemicals Ltd located in Shanghai, China, while glutathione was acquired from Chemical Reagents Ltd, also located in Shanghai, China. An analytically pure 10 mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was used as the experimental buffer. Ultrapure water was employed throughout the experiments, and all glassware was thoroughly washed thrice with aqua regia and ultrapure water.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was conducted utilizing an F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer and UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded utilizing a UV-2450 Shimadzu visible spectrometer, both from Hitachi, Japan. The zeta potentials of the nanoparticles were acquired utilizing a Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6701F, Japan) was used to characterize the morphology of the nanoparticles. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB250Xi, USA) of the nanoparticles was conducted utilizing Al K alpha X-ray radiation as the excitation source. Fluorescence lifetimes were analyzed at a FLS1000 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

CuNCs were synthesized by reducing CuSO4 with glutathione (GSH), following a method reported in the literature [33]. In brief, 10 mL of a 50 mg/mL GSH solution was added to 10 mL of a 10 mM CuSO4·5H2O solution and was stirred vigorously to form a white suspension at 37 °C for 1 h. The pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 5.0 with 1.0 M NaOH solution and kept stirring for 2 h. The solution gradually turned yellow and transparent during this process, indicating successful CuNCs synthesis. The prepared CuNCs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C and protected from light.

The ZIF/CuNCs were synthesized following the previous procedure [33]. Initially, 0.15 g of ZnAc2 and 0.33 g of 2-methylimidazole were dissolved in 7.5 mL of methanol, respectively. Subsequently, 1 mL of the CuNCs solution prepared by the above method and 7.5 mL of the methanolic solution of 2-methylimidazole were added in turn to the methanolic solution of ZnAc2 under vigorous stirring, then the mixture continued to be vigorously stirred for 10 min. The appearance of milky white color indicated the successful synthesis of ZIF/CuNCs nanomaterials. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged (5000 rpm, 10 min), followed by washing twice with methanol and the filter was dried under vacuum to obtain a white powder.

In this study, we synthesized bCDs through a hydrothermal method reported previously in the literature [34]. Initially, 0.24 g of melamine and 0.58 g of sodium citrate were dissolved simultaneously in 25 mL of ultrapure water. The solution underwent sonication for 5 min and was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, where it was kept for 12 h at 180 °C. After the reaction, the solution was filtered through a cylindrical membrane filter (0.22 μm) to remove macromolecules and later dialyzed using ultrapure water and a 500 Da dialysis membrane for 48 h. The dialyzed solution was then collected and preserved at 4 °C in a refrigerator with light-free.

Different concentrations of glyphosate (30 μL) and Cu2+ (10 μL, 1.0 mM) were mixed and swiftly chelated. Then, 50 μL of ZIF/CuNC solution was added, followed by the addition of 20 μL of bCDs solution after 10 min of reaction. The solution was adjusted to a final volume of 600 μL using PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0). Subsequently, fluorescence spectra were measured with excitation at 370 nm.

A series of different concentrations of glyphosate (30 μL) and bCDs/Cu2+ (15 μL, 1.0 mM) were mixed and rapidly chelated. Then, H2O2 (0.1 mM, 110 μL) and TMB (15 mM, 40 μL) were sequentially added to the solutions. The total volume of the solution was then adjusted to 600 μL through the addition of PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 4). The absorption spectra were subsequently recorded via a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of glyphosate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Actual samples of lake water, cucumber, and cabbage were collected from Lake Runxi at Nanchang University and a local farmers' market, respectively. A ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform based on ZIF/CuNCs nanomaterials combined with bCDs was utilized for detecting glyphosate in the samples. The samples were pretreated according to the process reported in literature [35]. Lake water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm polyethersulfone membrane needle filter to remove insoluble impurities. Cucumbers and Chinese cabbage were pulverized separately using a juicer, and then diluted with water in a 1:50 ratio. The samples were spiked with varying concentrations of glyphosate, and then subjected to centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min. After that, the filtrates were passed through a polysulfone membrane syringe (0.22 μm) for filtration. Finally, the glyphosate levels were analyzed in the filtrates by the previously mentioned detection method.

Characterization was carried out on the synthesized ZIF/CuNCs nanomaterial. The morphology of ZIF/CuNCs was characterized through SEM, as shown in Fig. 1 A, which revealed that the material was composed of large particles of 230 nm. In addition, SEM images of CuNCs, ZIF, and the mixture of bCDs with ZIF/CuNCs are presented in Figure S1. From the images, it could be see clearly that the CuNCs exhibit a nanocluster morphology, while the framework structure of ZIF presents as a dodecahedron. Upon the addition of bCDs to ZIF/CuNCs, the images show that bCDs are merely attached to the surface of ZIF/CuNCs. This phenomenon is due to the fact that no special treatment or reaction was applied to bCDs and ZIF/CuNCs, and they were simply mixed together. XPS spectra of ZIF/CuNCs showed the presence of Cu, Zn, O, C, N, and S (Fig. 1B). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Zn 2p (Fig. 1C) exhibited two main peaks at 1044.3 eV and 1021.3 eV, which were attributed to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively [36]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p (Fig. 1D) displayed two peaks at 952.1 eV and 932 eV, corresponded to Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2, respectively, which was consistent with the Cu (0) state [32]. There was no peak at around 942 eV, indicating the absence of Cu2+. The assembly mechanism between CuNCs and ZIF was studied by measuring Zeta potential. As shown in Fig. 1E, the average Zeta potentials of CuNCs and ZIF were −14.2 mV and 5.88 mV, respectively, indicating the presence of electrostatic attraction between them. In addition, the average Zeta potential of ZIF/CuNCs was determined for −7.68 mV, illustrating that electrostatic interactions caused the formation of ZIF/CuNCs. Fig. 1F showed that the peak corresponding to the -SH (2159 cm−1) group of GSH disappeared in the FT-IR spectrum of CuNCs after being encapsulated in ZIF, indicating the formation of a Cu-S covalent bond [37]. The FT-IR spectrum of ZIF/CuNCs exhibited the same characteristic absorption peaks as ZIF, also proving successful synthesis of the nanomaterial. To verify the fluorescence enhancement effect of CuNCs encapsulated in ZIF, we measured their fluorescence intensity separately. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced when CuNCs were encapsulated in ZIF (Fig. 1G). Finally, we explored the optimal excitation wavelength of bCDs (Fig. 1H), which was found to be 350 nm, and showed strong fluorescence emission at 440 nm.

A serial of relative experimental analyses were carried out in order to elucidate the sensing mechanism of this dual-signal sensing platform for quantitative analysis of glyphosate. To evaluate the peroxidase-like activity of bCDs/Cu2+ in the colorimetric sensor, we compared the steady-state kinetics of catalytic oxidation of TMB by Cu2+ alone and bCDs/Cu2+. According to the changes analysis in UV–visible absorbance as a function of reaction time at different concentrations of TMB and H2O2, the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk plots were obtained (Fig. 2 A and B). In the reaction of bCDs/Cu2+ with TMB, the Michaelis constant (K m) was calculated as 0.69 mM and the maximum reaction rate (V max) was 3.82 μM/min, while the corresponding K m and V max for individual bCDs were 0.87 mM and 3.14 μM/min, respectively. The smaller K m value shows that bCDs/Cu2+ has a higher binding affinity for TMB, while the larger V max value indicates that bCDs/Cu2+ has higher catalytic activity than bCDs. In addition, the K m and V max of bCDs/Cu2+ for H2O2 were 0.37 mM and 8.13 μM/min, respectively. Thus, bCDs/Cu2+ has higher binding affinity and catalytic activity for TMB than individual bCDs.

In the fluorescence sensing platform, as shown in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence lifetime of ZIF/CuNCs significantly decreased after combining with Cu2+, suggesting that Cu2+ is a dynamic quencher for ZIF/CuNCs. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a common dynamic quenching mechanism, which typically requires spectral overlap between the absorption of the acceptor and the emission of the donor [38]. However, as shown in Fig. 2D, there is no spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of ZIF/CuNCs and the absorption spectrum of Cu2+, eliminating the possibility of FRET. The quenching mechanism was further studied by XPS analysis. Fig. 2E shows the XPS spectrum of Cu 2p of ZIF/CuNCs after introducing Cu2+, which indicates that Cu2+ was fixed in ZIF/CuNCs, and thus formed a complex with the imidazole in ZIF, resulting in fluorescence quenching [39].

Based on this mechanism, we investigated the feasibility of the dual-signal sensor. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the bCDs/ZIF/CuNCs system exhibited strong fluorescence emission (curve a), and the effect of glyphosate on the fluorescence signal was negligible (curve b). However, when Cu2+ was involved, the fluorescence of ZIF/CuNCs was quenched (curve d). As glyphosate was continued to introduce the mixture solution, the fluorescence of ZIF/CuNCs was restored (curve c). In addition, the stable fluorescence signal of bCDs was explored as a reference. As shown in curve e, the fluorescence signal of bCDs in the system remained basically unchanged, and it can be used to generate a ratio fluorescence signal with ZIF/CuNCs. In Fig. 3B, the comparison of curves (a), (b) and (c) showed that bCDs/Cu2+ had the highest peroxidase-like activity, and the activity decreased when glyphosate was present, which was also reflected in the decreased UV absorbance of TMB (curve d). In conclusion, the dual-signal sensing platform developed in this study can be used for the detection of glyphosate.

To enhance the detection performance of the sensing platform, we optimized the conditions that might affect the sensor, such as Cu2+ concentration, the amount of ZIF/CuNCs, pH, and quenching time. In this study, we used the fluorescence intensity ratio (I630/I440) to measure the performance of the sensor, and (I630/I440)0 and (I630/I440)A were used to represent the solutions in the absence and presence of glyphosate, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2A, we explored the optimal pH range of the experiment within pH 6.0–8.0 and found that the best pH was 7.0. Under this condition, we further optimized the Cu2+ concentration and the amount of ZIF/CuNCs, and found that the optimal concentration of Cu2+ was 16.7 μM (Fig. S2B), and the optimal volume of ZIF/CuNCs was 50 μL (Fig. S2C). Finally, we found that the maximum quenching efficiency was reached when the quenching time was 10 min (Fig. S2D). In addition, we found that the fluorescence intensity of bCDs/ZIF/CuNCs decreased with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, as shown in Fig. S2E, and the fluorescence signal of bCDs/ZIF/CuNCs exhibited a good linear relationship with Cu2+ concentration within the range of 0.16–180 μM. The linear fit equation was Y = 0.017X + 0.0833, and R2 was 0.992 (Fig. S2F).

The assay conditions were systematically optimized considering that environmental factors, such as pH, bCDs/Cu2+ concentration, substrate concentration, incubation time, and temperature, can influence the catalytic activity of bCDs/Cu2+ and ultimately affect the sensing performance. Herein, the absorbance difference ΔA (ΔA = A0-A1) was used as a measurement scale, where A0 and A1 represent the absorbance of the system in the absence and presence of glyphosate, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S3A, we found that the optimal concentration of bCDs/Cu2+ was 0.15 mM, while the optimal concentrations of TMB and H2O2 were 15 mM (Fig. S3B) and 18.3 μM (Fig. S3C), respectively. In addition, the optimal pH of the reaction was investigated and it was found to be 4 (Fig. S3D). Finally, as illustrated in Fig. S3E and S3F, the maximum ΔA value was reached when the system was reacted at 20 °C for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of glyphosate with dual signal sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, the linear range and detection limit of the detection were explored by the dual-signal sensing platform for glyphosate. In Fig. 4 A, it can be seen that the fluorescence signal of bCDs/ZIF/CuNCs at 630 nm was gradually enhanced with increasing glyphosate concentration. Furthermore, the results showed a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the logarithm of the corresponding glyphosate concentration in the range of 0.5–230 ng/mL (R2 = 0.992). The calibration equation can be expressed as Y = 0.0043X + 0.794 (Fig. 4B), and the calculated LOD was 0.125 ng/mL based on 3σ/s (s is the slope of the curve and σ is the standard deviation). Besides, the fluorescence color change can be clearly observed from blue to red under the UV lamp in the inset of Fig. 4B. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 4C, the concentration of glyphosate in the range of 0.5–75 ng/mL showed a good linear correlation with the absorbance of TMB (R2 = 0.993). The linear regression equation obtained was Y = 0.0110X + 1.05 (Fig. 4D) with a calculated detection limit of 0.136 ng/mL. Moreover, a gradual lightening of the solution color can also be seen in the inset of Fig. 4D. Table 1 summarized the comparison of the sensing performances between other analytical methods [35–41] and the proposed method in this system for organophosphorus pesticides in recent years. This work has lower detection limits and more sensitive and accurate results under the dual-signal sensing strategy of fluorescence and colorimetry. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity and the absorbance of TMB were monitored every day for 7 days and the standard deviation was less 2.9 %. These results showed that the proposed method was stable with good repeatability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective exploration of dual-signal sensing platforms
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the sensor was assessed by evaluating its response to interfering substances that may be present in environmental and food samples. Metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+), inorganic salt ions (NH4 +, Cl-, NO3 –, HCO3 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, H2PO4 -), glucose, amino acids (glycine, cysteine), vitamins (ascorbic acid, vitamin B2) were included. These were selected as potential interfering substances because they might interact with the sensor in a way that alters its sensitivity or responsiveness, potentially leading to inaccurate measurements of glyphosate concentration. It's worth noting that these interfering substances primarily originate from natural processes such as the leaching of soil minerals, atmospheric deposition, photosynthesis, and microbial degradation in lakes and plants. Additionally, human activities, such as agricultural runoff and industrial wastewater discharge, also contribute to their presence in lake water and fruits and vegetables. In the experiment, glyphosate (25 ng/mL) was used as a blank control, and the interfering substances at a 100-fold higher concentration were added to the system separately. As can be seen in Fig. 5 A and B, there was no significant change in fluorescence intensity and absorption compared to the control group in the presence of various interfering substances.

We conducted measurements by the sensors on a variety of samples, including lake water, cucumber, and Chinese cabbage to estimate practical application capabilities for assay of glyphosate. The results were compiled in Table 2 and the fluorescence and colorimetric detection recoveries ranged from 91.63 % to 105.3 %, which indicates that the sensor demonstrated a high level of accuracy. Additionally, the relative standard deviations were within the range of 1.2 % to 6.1 %, demonstrating that the sensor exhibited good precision. Therefore, the dual-signal sensor thus holds great potential for detecting glyphosate in both food and environmental settings.

In this study, we incorporated CuNCs into the framework of ZIF to create stable and highly fluorescent ZIF/CuNCs nanomaterials. Using this material, we developed a versatile dual-signal sensing platform for sensitive and visual detection of glyphosate. The fluorescent ZIF/CuNCs sensor demonstrated a wide linear range and low-to-high fluorescence emission during glyphosate detection, with a detection limit as low as 0.125 ng/mL. Additionally, the TMB-mediated bCDs/Cu2+ color sensor showed good detection performance. The dual-signal sensing platform was successfully employed for the detection of glyphosate in environmental and food samples with satisfactory accuracy and precision. In view, this method offers advantages such as simplicity, stability, and ease of use, and most importantly, it allows for different reading modes to be selected based on different conditions. This feature enables reliable, sensitive, and rapid detection of glyphosate in various settings. However, the response time of the sensor was relatively long, and more effective methods need to be developed to achieve rapid detection of glyphosate.

Huiwen Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xukai Liu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiulin Wang: Data curation, Methodology, Resources. Ping Qiu: . Pengjun Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765015, 21808099), China and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123796.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Snowflake-like gold nanoparticles as SERS substrates for the sensitive detection of organophosphorus pesticide residues
